# AxioDB — Complete Documentation

Version: 12.11.27 | License: MIT | Node.js >= 20.0.0
npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/axiodb
GitHub: https://github.com/nexoral/AxioDB
Docs: https://axiodb.in

This file documents the actual, source-verified API surface of AxioDB — every method signature, every query/update/aggregation operator, and every constructor option that exists in the shipped code. Where an example below differs from what you may have seen elsewhere (including AxioDB's own README), this file is the corrected version; specific corrections are called out inline.

---

## What is AxioDB?

AxioDB is an embedded NoSQL database for Node.js with MongoDB-style queries. It is a pure JavaScript alternative to SQLite with zero native dependencies, no compilation step, and no platform-specific binaries. It works everywhere Node.js runs — Electron apps, CLI tools, local-first applications, and Node.js backends.

**One-liner:** `npm install axiodb` and you have a database. No mongod process, no node-gyp, no electron-rebuild.

**Sweet spot:** 10,000–500,000 documents per collection, single process.

**Not a fit for:** 10M+ documents, multi-user web apps with hundreds of concurrent connections, relational data with JOINs, distributed/replicated/sharded systems, or cross-collection ACID transactions (AxioDB's transactions are scoped to a single collection).

---

## Installation

```bash
npm install axiodb
```

Requires Node.js >= 20.0.0, npm >= 6.0.0. Server-side/desktop only — does not run in a browser (needs the filesystem).

---

## Quick Start

```javascript
const { AxioDB } = require('axiodb');

const db = new AxioDB({ GUI: true }); // GUI at http://localhost:27018

const myDB = await db.createDB('HelloWorldDB');
const collection = await myDB.createCollection('greetings');

await collection.insert({ message: 'Hello, Developer!' });

const result = await collection.query({}).exec();
console.log(result.data.documents[0].message); // Hello, Developer!
```

`AxioDB` is a hard singleton — only one instance per process. Constructing a second one throws `Only one instance of AxioDB is allowed.`

---

## AxioDB (root instance)

```typescript
new AxioDB(options?: {
  GUI?: boolean;          // Enable HTTP GUI on port 27018 (default: false)
  RootName?: string;      // Root storage folder name (default: "AxioDB")
  CustomPath?: string;    // Custom storage path (default: current working directory)
  TCP?: boolean;          // Enable AxioDBCloud TCP server on port 27019 (default: false)
  TCPAuth?: boolean;      // Require username/password on TCP connections, same RBAC as GUI (default: false)
  TLS?: boolean;          // Encrypt the TCP server with TLS (default: false)
  TLSCertPath?: string;   // Path to a PEM cert file - required if TLS: true
  TLSKeyPath?: string;    // Path to the matching PEM private key - required if TLS: true
})
```

If `TLS: true` but either path is missing/unreadable, the constructor throws immediately — it never silently falls back to plaintext.

```typescript
db.createDB(dbName: string): Promise<Database>
db.deleteDatabase(dbName: string): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
db.isDatabaseExists(dbName: string): Promise<boolean>
db.getInstanceInfo(): Promise<SuccessInterface | undefined>
```

There is no schema-validation parameter on `createDB` or `createCollection` — AxioDB is schema-less. (If you've seen a `createDB(name, schemaValidation)` or `createCollection(name, enc, key, schema)` signature anywhere, that's not a real feature.)

---

## Database

```typescript
database.createCollection(
  name: string,
): Promise<Collection>

database.deleteCollection(name: string): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
database.isCollectionExists(name: string): Promise<boolean>
database.getCollectionInfo(): Promise<SuccessInterface | undefined>
```

---

## Collection

```typescript
collection.insert(document: object): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
collection.insertMany(documents: object[] | object): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
collection.query(query: object): Reader
collection.update(query: object): UpdateOperation
collection.delete(query: object): DeleteOperation
collection.aggregate(pipeline: object[]): Aggregation
collection.newIndex(...fieldNames: string[]): Promise<SuccessInterface>
collection.dropIndex(indexName: string): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
collection.getIndexes(): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
collection.totalDocuments(): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
collection.beginTransaction(): Transaction
collection.startSession(options?: SessionOptions): Session
```

`query()`, `update()`, `delete()`, and `aggregate()` all return chainable objects — nothing happens until you call the terminal method (`.exec()`, `.UpdateOne()`, `.deleteMany()`, etc.). There is no `collection.findAll()` method.

`insert()` is routed through a single-operation transaction internally, so it is Write-Ahead-Log-backed: if the process crashes mid-write, the next collection open (`Transaction.recoverTransactions()`, run automatically on `Collection` construction) redoes or undoes the half-finished write from the WAL.

### Insert

```javascript
await users.insert({ name: 'Alice', email: 'alice@example.com', age: 30 });

await users.insertMany([
  { name: 'Bob', age: 25 },
  { name: 'Carol', age: 35 },
]);
```

`documentId` and `updatedAt` are set automatically — don't set them yourself, they'll be overwritten.

---

## Query (Reader)

```typescript
collection.query(query: object): Reader

reader.Limit(n: number): Reader
reader.Skip(n: number): Reader
reader.Sort(sort: object): Reader            // { field: 1 } ascending, { field: -1 } descending
reader.setCount(count: boolean): Reader      // include totalDocuments in the result
reader.setProject(fields: object): Reader    // { field: 1 } to include only that field
reader.findOne(matchOneOnly?: boolean): Reader  // still needs .exec() after - see below
reader.exec(): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
```

`findOne()` returns `Reader` for chaining, not a Promise directly — you must still call `.exec()`:

```javascript
const one = await users.query({ email: 'alice@example.com' }).findOne(true).exec();
```

```javascript
const all = await users.query({}).exec();

const paginated = await users
  .query({ role: 'admin' })
  .Sort({ age: -1 })
  .Limit(10)
  .Skip(20)
  .setProject({ name: 1, email: 1 })
  .exec();

const withCount = await users.query({ role: 'admin' }).setCount(true).exec();
```

### Query operators (full list)

These work on any field passed to `.query()` — this is the complete set as implemented, more than what's usually documented:

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `$gt` | Greater than | `{ age: { $gt: 25 } }` |
| `$gte` | Greater than or equal | `{ age: { $gte: 18 } }` |
| `$lt` | Less than | `{ age: { $lt: 60 } }` |
| `$lte` | Less than or equal | `{ price: { $lte: 100 } }` |
| `$eq` | Equal | `{ status: { $eq: 'active' } }` |
| `$ne` | Not equal | `{ status: { $ne: 'inactive' } }` |
| `$in` | Value in array | `{ role: { $in: ['admin', 'mod'] } }` |
| `$nin` | Value not in array | `{ tag: { $nin: ['spam'] } }` |
| `$exists` | Field present/absent | `{ email: { $exists: true } }` |
| `$regex` | Regular expression (use `$options` for flags, default `'i'`) | `{ name: { $regex: '^John', $options: 'i' } }` or a real `RegExp` |
| `$not` | Negates a nested condition | `{ age: { $not: { $lt: 18 } } }` |
| `$type` | Value's JS type (`'string'`, `'number'`, `'array'`, `'null'`, ...) | `{ age: { $type: 'number' } }` |
| `$size` | Array length equals | `{ tags: { $size: 3 } }` |
| `$all` | Array contains every given value | `{ tags: { $all: ['a', 'b'] } }` |
| `$elemMatch` | At least one array element matches a nested query | `{ items: { $elemMatch: { qty: { $gt: 5 } } } }` |
| `$or` | Logical OR (top-level) | `{ $or: [{ age: { $lt: 18 } }, { age: { $gt: 65 } }] }` |
| `$and` | Logical AND (top-level) | `{ $and: [{ age: { $gt: 18 } }, { active: true }] }` |
| `$nor` | None of the given conditions match (top-level) | `{ $nor: [{ status: 'banned' }, { status: 'deleted' }] }` |

`$gt`/`$gte`/`$lt`/`$lte` can be combined on the same field (e.g. a range query `{ age: { $gte: 18, $lte: 65 } }`). `documentId` and single-field exact-match queries on an indexed field use the index for O(1) lookup; everything else falls back to a full collection scan.

---

## Update

```typescript
collection.update(query: object): UpdateOperation

updater.Sort(sort: object): UpdateOperation
updater.UpdateOne(data: object): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>   // first match only
updater.UpdateMany(data: object): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface> // every match
```

```javascript
await users.update({ name: 'Alice' }).UpdateOne({ role: 'superadmin' });
await users.update({ role: 'trial' }).UpdateMany({ role: 'active' });
```

**Important correction:** updates are a **flat merge** — `data` is shallow-merged onto the existing document (`{ ...oldDoc, ...data }`). There is no MongoDB-style update-operator support (`$inc`, `$set`, `$push`, `$pull`, `$unset`, etc.). If you write `.UpdateOne({ $inc: { balance: -100 } })`, it does **not** increment `balance` — it literally sets a field named `"$inc"` to `{ balance: -100 }` on the document. To increment a value, read it and write the new value yourself:

```javascript
const doc = await users.query({ name: 'Alice' }).exec();
const newBalance = doc.data.documents[0].balance - 100;
await users.update({ name: 'Alice' }).UpdateOne({ balance: newBalance });
```

Updating a document only touches the index entries for fields whose value actually changed (not every indexed field on the document), so unrelated indexed fields keep their existing order.

---

## Delete

```typescript
collection.delete(query: object): DeleteOperation

deleter.Sort(sort: object): DeleteOperation
deleter.deleteOne(): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>   // first match only
deleter.deleteMany(): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface> // every match
```

```javascript
await users.delete({ name: 'Dave' }).deleteOne();
await users.delete({ active: false }).deleteMany();
```

---

## Aggregation

```typescript
collection.aggregate(pipeline: object[]): Aggregation

aggregation.exec(): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
```

```javascript
const stats = await users.aggregate([
  { $match: { active: true } },
  { $group: { _id: '$role', count: { $sum: 1 }, avgAge: { $avg: '$age' } } },
  { $sort: { count: -1 } },
  { $project: { role: 1, count: 1 } },
  { $limit: 10 },
]).exec();
```

**Hard requirement:** the pipeline's first stage must contain a `$match` key (even `{ $match: {} }` for "match everything") — a pipeline that doesn't start with `$match` throws `Pipeline must have a $match operator at top`.

Supported stages, in the order they're applied within a pipeline:

- **`$match`** — filters documents. Its operator set is **narrower** than `.query()`'s: exact equality (string/number/boolean), a `RegExp` instance or `{ $regex, $options }`, `$gte`, `$gt`, `$lte`, `$lt`, `$ne`, `$in`, `$nin`. No `$exists`, `$or`, `$and`, `$nor`, `$elemMatch`, `$not`, `$type`, `$size`, `$all`, or `$eq` inside `$match` — those are `.query()`-only.
- **`$group`** — groups by `_id` (a literal value, `'$fieldName'`, or an object of field-path expressions). Accumulators: `$sum` and `$avg` only (no `$min`, `$max`, `$push`, `$first`, `$last`).
- **`$sort`** — `{ field: 1 | -1 }`, single field per stage.
- **`$project`** — inclusion-only: `{ field: 1 }` keeps `field`; there's no exclusion (`0`) or computed/renamed fields.
- **`$limit`** — `{ $limit: n }`.
- **`$skip`** — `{ $skip: n }`.
- **`$unwind`** — `{ $unwind: '$arrayField' }`, deconstructs an array field into one document per element.
- **`$addFields`** — `{ $addFields: { field: literalValue } }`, a plain merge — not expression-evaluated.

---

## Transactions

Transactions are ACID and scoped to a single collection, with savepoints, rollback, and Write-Ahead Logging for crash recovery.

```typescript
collection.beginTransaction(): Transaction

transaction.insert(data: object): Transaction               // chainable, queues the op
transaction.update(query: object, data: object): Transaction
transaction.delete(query: object): Transaction
transaction.savepoint(name: string): Transaction
transaction.rollbackTo(name: string): Transaction
transaction.releaseSavepoint(name: string): Transaction
transaction.commit(): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
transaction.rollback(): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
```

Same flat-merge caveat as regular `Update`: `transaction.update(query, data)` shallow-merges `data` onto the existing document — no `$inc`/`$set` operators here either.

```javascript
const tx = collection.beginTransaction();
try {
  tx.insert({ name: 'Charlie', balance: 500 });
  tx.savepoint('checkpoint1');
  tx.update({ name: 'Alice' }, { active: false });
  const result = await tx.commit();
} catch (err) {
  await tx.rollback();
}
```

`commit()`'s success payload includes `documentIds: string[]` — the documentId(s) of any `insert()` operations in that transaction.

---

## Session

MongoDB-like session wrapper around `Transaction`, adding automatic retry.

```typescript
collection.startSession(options?: {
  defaultTimeout?: number;  // ms, default 60000
  retryWrites?: boolean;    // default true
  maxRetries?: number;      // default 3
}): Session

session.getSessionId(): string
session.isSessionActive(): boolean
session.startTransaction(): Transaction   // only one active transaction per session at a time
session.withTransaction(callback: (txn: Transaction) => Promise<void>): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
session.commitTransaction(): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
session.abortTransaction(): Promise<SuccessInterface | ErrorInterface>
session.endSession(): Promise<void>
```

```javascript
const session = collection.startSession();
await session.withTransaction(async (tx) => {
  tx.insert({ name: 'Alice', balance: 1000 });
  tx.update({ name: 'Bob' }, { active: true });
  // Auto-commits on success, auto-rolls-back on error
});
```

`withTransaction` automatically retries (exponential backoff, up to `maxRetries`) when the failure looks transient — its message contains `timeout`, `lock`, `deadlock`, `conflict`, `busy`, `temporary`, `EBUSY`, or `EAGAIN` (case-insensitive). Anything else fails immediately, no retry.

---

## Indexing

```javascript
await users.newIndex('email');                  // single field
await users.newIndex('email', 'age', 'role');    // multiple fields, one call
await users.dropIndex('email');
await users.getIndexes();
```

`documentId` is always indexed automatically — no setup needed, O(1) lookups. Every index is dual-written (in-memory cache + disk); the in-memory copy has a randomized 5–15 minute TTL (staggered on purpose, to avoid a cache-stampede when many entries would otherwise expire at once) and reloads from disk on a cold start or cache miss. A query that isn't an indexed exact match, or whose index misses, falls back to a full collection scan.

---

## Built-in Web GUI & RBAC

```javascript
const db = new AxioDB({ GUI: true }); // http://localhost:27018
```

Features: visual database/collection browser, real-time data inspection, query execution UI, performance monitoring, index management API (`GET /api/index/list`, `POST /api/index/create`, `DELETE /api/index/delete`).

On first start (`GUI: true` or `TCP: true` + `TCPAuth: true`), AxioDB seeds a reserved `config` database (hidden from the normal database list, and blocked from being read/written directly — `403 "This is a reserved system database"`) with a default account:

```
Username: admin
Password: admin
```

Forced password change on first login, for every account, not just the default one — no way around it other than completing it via the GUI. Predefined roles:

| Role | Access |
|------|--------|
| Super Admin | Full access, including user/role management |
| Admin | Full database/collection/document access, no user/role management |
| View | Read-only |

Login rate limiting: 5 failed attempts from one IP within a trailing 15-minute window → 15-minute lockout (`429`), shared between the GUI and TCP `AUTHENTICATE`. Sessions live only in server memory (never persisted) — restarting the server logs everyone out.

---

## AxioDBCloud — Remote TCP Access

Same API as embedded AxioDB, over a TCP connection — 35+ commands (full CRUD, aggregation, indexing).

### Server

```javascript
const { AxioDB } = require('axiodb');
const db = new AxioDB({ TCP: true, RootName: 'MyDB', CustomPath: '.' });          // plaintext, no auth
const db = new AxioDB({ TCP: true, TCPAuth: true, RootName: 'MyDB', CustomPath: '.' }); // auth required, shares GUI's RBAC
const db = new AxioDB({ TCP: true, TLS: true, TLSCertPath: './cert.pem', TLSKeyPath: './key.pem' }); // TLS-encrypted
```

### Client

```typescript
new AxioDBCloud(connectionString: string, options?: {
  timeout?: number;              // request timeout, ms (default: 30000)
  reconnectAttempts?: number;    // default: 10
  reconnectDelay?: number;       // initial backoff, ms (default: 1000)
  heartbeatInterval?: number;    // PING/PONG interval, ms (default: 30000)
  username?: string;             // if server has TCPAuth: true
  password?: string;
  maxPoolSize?: number;          // concurrent pooled connections (default: 10, mirrors MongoDB driver option)
  tls?: boolean;                 // must match server's TLS setting
  tlsCAPath?: string;            // CA/self-signed cert to validate server against, when tls: true
  tlsRejectUnauthorized?: boolean; // default true - only disable for local/dev, never production
})

client.connect(): Promise<void>          // must be awaited before any other call
client.disconnect(): Promise<void>
client.login(username: string, password: string): Promise<AuthenticatedUser>
client.authenticatedUser: AuthenticatedUser | undefined   // getter
client.sendCommand(command: string, params: any): Promise<any>  // low-level escape hatch
client.createDB(name: string): Promise<DatabaseProxy>
client.deleteDatabase(name: string): Promise<void>
client.isDatabaseExists(name: string): Promise<boolean>
client.getInstanceInfo(): Promise<any>
client.on('poolDegraded', (event: { requested, connected, failed, errors }) => {})
client.on('error', (err) => {})
```

```javascript
const { AxioDBCloud } = require('axiodb');

const client = new AxioDBCloud('axiodb://localhost:27019', { username: 'admin', password: 'admin' });
await client.connect(); // auto-authenticates since credentials were passed in the constructor

const db = await client.createDB('ProductionDB');
const users = await db.createCollection('Users');
await users.insert({ name: 'Alice' });
const results = await users.query({ role: 'admin' }).Limit(10).exec();

await client.disconnect();
```

`connect()` resolves even if the pool comes up smaller than `maxPoolSize` requested (emits `poolDegraded`) — as long as at least one pool member connected. If the very first connection fails, `connect()` rejects outright.

**DoS protections (server-side, not configurable at runtime):** max 1,000 total concurrent connections; max 100 concurrent connections per IP (`429`); max 300 connection attempts per IP per 10s, else 30s cooldown (`429`).

**TLS:** off by default, plaintext wire protocol otherwise. You must supply your own cert/key (AxioDB never generates one). Self-signed certs need `tlsCAPath` on the client to be trusted, or the client refuses to connect by default.

---

## Docker Deployment

```bash
docker run -d \
  --name axiodb-server \
  -p 27018:27018 -p 27019:27019 \
  -e AXIODB_TCP_AUTH=true \
  -v axiodb-data:/app \
  theankansaha/axiodb
```

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `AXIODB_GUI` | `true` | HTTP GUI on port 27018 |
| `AXIODB_TCP` | `true` | AxioDBCloud TCP server on port 27019 |
| `AXIODB_TCP_AUTH` | `true` | Require TCP authentication (shared RBAC with GUI) |
| `AXIODB_TLS` | `false` | Encrypt TCP with TLS |
| `AXIODB_TLS_CERT_PATH` | *(none)* | In-container path to PEM cert — required if `AXIODB_TLS=true` |
| `AXIODB_TLS_KEY_PATH` | *(none)* | In-container path to PEM key — required if `AXIODB_TLS=true` |
| `AXIODB_ROOT_NAME` | `AxioDB` | Root storage folder name |
| `AXIODB_CUSTOM_PATH` | container's working dir | Storage path inside the container |
| `AXIODB_MCP` | `false` | Enable the MCP server on port 27020 |
| `AXIODB_MCP_PORT` | `27020` | MCP server port |

Ports (27018/27019) aren't env-configurable — remap at the Docker layer (`-p host:container`). TCP auth is **on by default** in the published image; complete the `admin`/`admin` forced password change via the GUI before connecting over TCP.

`UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE` matters under concurrent disk I/O — the published image auto-computes it from the container's actual CPU allotment (`4 × allotted CPUs`, clamped `[4, 64]`); override with `-e UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE=16` if needed.

---

## MCP Server — AI Agent Integration

```bash
docker run -d -e AXIODB_GUI=true -e AXIODB_MCP=true \
  -p 27018:27018 -p 27019:27019 -p 27020:27020 \
  -v axiodb-data:/app theankansaha/axiodb
```

32 tools at `http://localhost:27020/mcp` covering databases, collections, documents, aggregation, indexes, dashboard stats, and user/role management — gated by the same RBAC as the GUI (a View-role session gets a real `403` on write tools). Every tool except `axiodb_login` requires a `sessionId` from logging in first. Compatible with Claude Code, Codex CLI, opencode, GitHub Copilot CLI, Cursor, Windsurf, and Google Antigravity — see https://axiodb.in/mcp-server for exact registration commands per tool.

---

## Troubleshooting

- **"Not connected to server" right after `connect()`:** `connect()` is async — always `await` it before using the client.
- **`401` Authentication required:** a command was sent before a successful `AUTHENTICATE`. Pass `{ username, password }` in the constructor, or call `client.login(...)` first.
- **`403` must change password before TCP use:** applies to every account on first use, including a freshly created one. Complete it via the GUI, then reconnect.
- **`429` too many failed logins:** 5 failures/IP/15min → 15-minute lockout, shared between GUI and TCP.
- **`429` too many concurrent connections:** per-IP cap is 100 concurrent sockets, independent of authentication.
- **`429` too many connection attempts:** per-IP churn guard, 300 attempts/10s → 30s cooldown.
- **`403` reserved system database:** the `config` database is AxioDB's own RBAC storage — use a different name.
- **Connection refused/timeout:** confirm `TCP: true` (or `AXIODB_TCP=true`) and the port is published/reachable; a protocol error mentioning "HTTP data on TCP port" usually means you're pointed at 27018 (GUI) instead of 27019 (TCP).
- **`EMFILE`/`ENFILE` at high concurrency:** each client opens `maxPoolSize` sockets; raise `ulimit -n` (bare-metal) or `--ulimit nofile=65536:65536` (Docker) — the published image already raises its own soft limit.

---

## Performance (10,000-document benchmark, illustrative — not a guarantee)

- Single insert: ~3ms · InsertMany (10 docs): ~87ms
- `documentId` lookup (indexed): ~1ms · `findOne`: ~1ms
- Query with `$gt`: ~469ms · Query with `$in`: ~401ms · Sort: ~382ms · Full scan: ~2.6s
- `UpdateOne`: ~8ms · `UpdateMany` (full collection): ~466ms
- `deleteOne`: ~3ms · `deleteMany`: ~446ms
- Transaction insert: ~23ms · Transaction update: ~14ms · Rollback: ~3ms

---

## Comparisons

### vs SQLite / better-sqlite3

| | SQLite / better-sqlite3 | AxioDB |
|---|---|---|
| Native dependencies | Yes (C bindings) | None |
| Query language | SQL strings | JS objects |
| Schema migrations | Required | Schema-less |
| Built-in caching | No | Yes |
| Multi-core | No | Worker Threads |
| Built-in GUI | No | Yes |
| Best for | 10M+ records, relational | 10K–500K docs, embedded |

### vs lowdb / nedb

| | lowdb | nedb | AxioDB |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maintained | Yes | No (abandoned) | Yes |
| Native bindings | None | None | None |
| Storage | Single JSON file | Single file/in-memory | File-per-document |
| Query language | JS/Lodash | JS objects | JS objects (MongoDB-style) |
| Built-in caching | No | No | Yes |
| Worker Threads | No | No | Yes |
| ACID transactions | No | No | Yes |
| TypeScript | Yes | Partial | Full |
| Sweet spot | <5K docs | <100K docs | 10K–500K docs |
| Built-in GUI | No | No | Yes |

### vs MongoDB

| | MongoDB | AxioDB |
|---|---|---|
| Server required | Yes | No — embedded |
| Network access | Yes | Optional (AxioDBCloud) |
| Scale | Distributed, unlimited | Single node, 10K–500K docs |
| Best for | Multi-user production | Desktop apps, CLI tools |

---

## Limitations

- Optimized for 10K–500K documents — use PostgreSQL/MongoDB/SQLite beyond that.
- Single-instance architecture — not for hundreds of concurrent web connections.
- Document-based, no JOINs or foreign keys.
- Single-node only — no replication, sharding, clustering.
- Transactions are scoped to a single collection.

None of this is a shortcoming to apologize for — it's a deliberately scoped embedded/local-first niche. Outgrowing it is a sign to migrate, not a bug.

---

## FAQ

**Is AxioDB a MongoDB replacement?** No — AxioDB is embedded (runs inside your app), MongoDB is client-server. Use AxioDB for desktop/CLI/local-first up to ~500K documents.

**Does it work with Electron?** Yes, it's the primary use case — zero native deps means no electron-rebuild, no `.node` files.

**Does update support `$inc`/`$set`/other MongoDB update operators?** No. Update data is a flat, shallow merge onto the existing document. See the Update section above.

**How many documents can it handle?** Optimized for 10K–500K. Use PostgreSQL/MongoDB for 1M+.

**TypeScript support?** Full, built-in — no separate `@types` package.

**Browser support?** No — Node.js (v20+) and filesystem access only.

**What is AxioDBCloud?** TCP-based remote client with the exact same API as embedded AxioDB — 1,000+ concurrent connections, auto-reconnect, optional TLS and shared-RBAC authentication.

---

MIT. Author: Ankan Saha (https://github.com/AnkanSaha). Organization: Nexoral (https://github.com/nexoral).
Security issues: see SECURITY.md in the GitHub repository — do not open public issues for vulnerabilities.
